context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Initiatives, Pathways and Stressors

Recommended due date: July 25 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will build on your Week 6 research by analyzing how your city's climate initiatives connect to transformation pathways and address environmental stressors.

Assignment Task
Complete the Initiatives, Pathways and Stressors Table using your Week 6 findings.

Step 1: List Key Initiatives
Identify the key initiatives and actions being proposed and implemented in your case study city (from your Week 6 research).

Step 2: Connect to Pathways
For each initiative, identify which of the 6 Pathways for Urban Transformation it relates to.

Step 3: Link to Stressors
Identify which environmental stressors each initiative addresses.

Resources
See the example provided in the course materials of a completed Initiatives, Pathways and Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i need help w/ the city case study table rn. i’m kinda stuck on matching my city’s initiatives to the transformation pathways and stressors.
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through it! Let's start with what you already have. Which city are you working with, and what initiatives did you identify in your Week 6 research?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting the conversation and has identified where they are stuck. Before providing any scaffolding, I should first find out what they already have - which city they chose and what initiatives they've identified - so I can guide them from there. This avoids over-scaffolding and lets me assess where they actually need help.

exchanges.student: i’m using jakarta, and i have a few things like flood control/coastal protection, public transit expansion, and maybe waste management stuff. idk if i’m grouping the initiatives right tho.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a solid set of initiatives to work with. Before we talk about groupings, let's make sure you're connecting to the right framework. What are the 6 Pathways for Urban Transformation from your course materials? Which ones do you remember?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good starting point with three initiative areas. Rather than validating or correcting their groupings right away, I should push them to engage with the course material first - specifically the 6 Pathways for Urban Transformation - before we discuss whether the groupings are right. This uses guided discovery and the Socratic method to get them to connect their own knowledge to the framework.

exchanges.student: ngl i only remember them vaguely 😅 i think one is about infrastructure and maybe one about governance? i’m blanking on the full 6.
exchanges.tutor: That's ok, but you'll need those pathways to complete the table - they're the core framework for this assignment. Go back to your course materials and look them up. Once you have the full list in front of you, come back and we can start matching them to Jakarta's initiatives.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student doesn't remember the 6 pathways, which are foundational to completing this assignment. I shouldn't list them out for the student - that would be giving them the answer. Instead, I should redirect them to their course materials, since these are clearly defined in the course. This keeps the student doing the work and ensures they engage with the actual content.

exchanges.student: ok yeah fair, i’ll pull them up. once i have the list, can u help me check if i’m matching jakarta’s flood and transit stuff right?
exchanges.tutor: Sure - once you have the list, make your best attempt at matching first, then share your reasoning with me. I'll help you think it through from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for confirmation in advance, which is fine - I can acknowledge the plan without committing to giving them answers. I should encourage them to come back with their attempt at matching first, so that I can then use the Socratic method to probe their reasoning rather than validating their choices directly.

exchanges.student: yeah that works, i’ll try it first. i just don’t wanna force an initiative into the wrong pathway lol.
exchanges.tutor: That concern makes sense, but the best way to find out is to try. Go look up the pathways and take a stab at it - wrong first attempts are part of the process. Come back with what you have.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing a reasonable concern but it's somewhat pre-emptive - they haven't even tried yet. I should briefly reassure them that the process of reasoning through it is the point, and nudge them to go do the work and come back with an attempt. No need for more scaffolding at this stage.

exchanges.student: got it, i’ll do a rough match and bring it back. hopefully it’s not totally off fr.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good - see you when you have something to show!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just wrapping up before going to do the work. No need for a lengthy response - just encourage them to come back with their attempt. Keep it brief.

exchanges.student: kk, brb once i’ve got the pathway list + my guesses.
exchanges.tutor: Take your time, see you soon!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just signing off briefly. A short acknowledgment is sufficient here - no need to add anything pedagogically substantive.

exchanges.student: thx, i’ll be back in a bit.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just confirming they'll be back. A minimal acknowledgment is appropriate here.
The student is still just in small talk mode. No pedagogical action needed - just wait for them to return with their work.

exchanges.student: i’m back lol. i found the pathways, but i’m still not sure how specific my initiatives should be for jakarta.
exchanges.tutor: Good, welcome back! Before we talk about specificity, share what you've got - your initiatives, the pathways you matched them to, and your reasoning. Let's work from something concrete.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is back but asking a vague question about specificity rather than showing me their actual attempt at matching. Per the rules, I should not let the student lead with abstract questions before showing their work. I should push them to share their actual matching attempt first so I can use the Socratic method on concrete material rather than answering a general question.
